title: Crataegus velata
taxonomy: Crataegus velata is classified in the kingdom Plantae, with more specific classification as follows: it belongs to the family Rosaceae, order Rosales, class Magnoliopsida, subclass Rosidae, and division Magnoliophyta. This classification reflects the plant's characteristics and relationships within the plant kingdom. The family Rosaceae includes a wide range of flowering plants, including roses and apples. The order Rosales is a group of flowering plants with a similar leaf structure, and the class Magnoliopsida includes plants with two cotyledons in their seeds. The classification of Crataegus velata within these higher-level categories provides a comprehensive understanding of its evolutionary relationships and characteristics.
uses: Crataegus velata, also known as the Beadle hawthorn, has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. The plant's bark, leaves, and fruit are used in various remedies. Its extracts are believed to have antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. In traditional medicine, Crataegus velata is used to treat conditions such as digestive issues, fever, and respiratory problems. However, further research is needed to confirm the plant's efficacy and safety for human consumption.

intro: Crataegus velata, commonly known as the hawthorn tree, is a deciduous tree or shrub belonging to the Rosaceae family, native to North America. The health benefits of Crataegus velata include its potential to support cardiovascular health, reduce blood pressure, and improve symptoms of anxiety and insomnia due to its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, oligomeric proanthocyanidins (OPCs), and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with Crataegus velata are tinctures, capsules, and teas, often used to support cardiovascular health and promote overall well-being.
